lesson.title: CAPS.PhysicalSciences.Gr12.P2.ECHEM: Galvanic cells: spontaneous redox reactions, cell notatio...
lesson.explanation: Galvanic cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy using spontaneous redox reactions. The more reactive metal is the anode (oxidation), the less reactive is the cathode (reduction). E¬įcell = E¬įcathode ‚ąí E¬įanode (must be positive for spontaneous). Electrolytic cells use external voltage to drive non-spontaneous reactions. In electrolysis: anode = oxidation (+), cathode = reduction (‚ąí) ‚ÄĒ opposite to galvanic.

examples.problem: Calculate E¬įcell for Zn|Zn¬≤‚Āļ||Cu¬≤‚Āļ|Cu. Given: E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V, E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Zn is more reactive ‚Üí anode (oxidation). Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu at cathode (reduction)

examples.problem: Calculate E¬įcell for Zn|Zn¬≤‚Āļ||Cu¬≤‚Āļ|Cu. Given: E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V, E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Zn is more reactive ‚Üí anode (oxidation). Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu at cathode (reduction)

examples.problem: Calculate E¬įcell for Zn|Zn¬≤‚Āļ||Cu¬≤‚Āļ|Cu. Given: E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V, E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Zn is more reactive ‚Üí anode (oxidation). Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu at cathode (reduction)

practice_problems.question: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of electron transfer.
practice_problems.answer: Oxidation is the loss of electrons. Reduction is the gain of electrons. (Remember: OIL RIG ‚ÄĒ Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.)
practice_problems.explanation: In redox reactions, electrons transfer from the substance being oxidised to the substance being reduced.

practice_problems.question: Calculate E¬įcell for Zn|Zn¬≤‚Āļ||Cu¬≤‚Āļ|Cu. Given: E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V, E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.
hints: Zn is the anode (more reactive, lower E¬į). Cu is the cathode.
practice_problems.explanation: E¬įcell = E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) ‚ąí E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = 0.34 ‚ąí (‚ąí0.76) = 1.10 V. Positive, so spontaneous.

practice_problems.question: Write the cell notation for a galvanic cell using Mg and Ag half-cells. Given: E¬į(Mg¬≤‚Āļ/Mg) = ‚ąí2.37 V, E¬į(Ag‚Āļ/Ag) = +0.80 V.
hints: The more reactive metal (lower E¬į) is the anode, written on the left.
Single line | = phase boundary. Double line || = salt bridge.
practice_problems.explanation: Mg is more reactive (lower E¬į), so it is oxidised at the anode. Cell notation: anode|anode ion||cathode ion|cathode.

practice_problems.question: In a galvanic cell, explain the function of the salt bridge.
practice_problems.answer: The salt bridge allows ions to flow between the two half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality. Without it, charge builds up (excess positive in cathode, excess negative in anode) and the cell stops working.
hints: What happens to the charge balance as the cell operates?
Cations move toward the cathode compartment, anions toward the anode.
practice_problems.explanation: As Cu¬≤‚Āļ is reduced, the cathode solution loses positive ions. The salt bridge supplies K‚Āļ or Na‚Āļ ions. In the anode, excess Zn¬≤‚Āļ is balanced by Cl‚ĀĽ or NO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ from the salt bridge.

practice_problems.question: In the electrolysis of CuSO‚āĄ solution with copper electrodes, describe what happens at each electrode and how the mass of each electrode changes.
practice_problems.answer: Anode: Cu ‚Üí Cu¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ (copper dissolves, mass decreases). Cathode: Cu¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí Cu (copper deposits, mass increases). The concentration of CuSO‚āĄ solution remains constant.
hints: At which electrode does oxidation occur?
Where do the Cu¬≤‚Āļ ions in solution come from?
practice_problems.explanation: Copper transfers from anode to cathode through the solution. This is the principle behind copper refining (electroplating).
distractors: Oxygen is produced at the cathode

practice_problems.question: Using the table of standard reduction potentials, determine whether the reaction Fe(s) + Cu¬≤‚Āļ(aq) ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ(aq) + Cu(s) is spontaneous. E¬į(Fe¬≤‚Āļ/Fe) = ‚ąí0.44 V, E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.
practice_problems.answer: E¬įcell = E¬įcathode ‚ąí E¬įanode = 0.34 ‚ąí (‚ąí0.44) = +0.78 V. Since E¬įcell > 0, the reaction is spontaneous.
hints: Identify which species is oxidised and which is reduced.
If E¬įcell is positive, the reaction is spontaneous.
practice_problems.explanation: Fe is oxidised (anode), Cu¬≤‚Āļ is reduced (cathode). E¬įcell = +0.78 V > 0 ‚Üí spontaneous. Fe will dissolve in CuSO‚āĄ solution and copper will deposit.

practice_problems.question: Compare a galvanic cell and an electrolytic cell in terms of: (a) energy conversion, (b) spontaneity, (c) anode sign, and (d) one industrial application of each.
practice_problems.answer: (a) Galvanic: chemical ‚Üí electrical. Electrolytic: electrical ‚Üí chemical. (b) Galvanic: spontaneous (E¬įcell > 0). Electrolytic: non-spontaneous (requires external voltage). (c) Galvanic: anode is negative. Electrolytic: anode is positive. (d) Galvanic: batteries. Electrolytic: electroplating or aluminium extraction.
hints: Think about which cell generates electricity and which uses it.
In both cells: oxidation occurs at the anode, but the sign of the anode differs.
practice_problems.explanation: Both cells have oxidation at anode and reduction at cathode (AN OX, RED CAT). The key difference is energy source and spontaneity.
distractors: Electrolytic cells don't need a power supply

hints: Remember: AN OX, RED CAT ‚ÄĒ Anode = Oxidation, Reduction = Cathode. In galvanic cells, anode is negative; in electrolytic, anode is positive.
Remember: AN OX, RED CAT ‚ÄĒ Anode = Oxidation, Reduction = Cathode. In galvanic cells, anode is negative; in electrolytic, anode is positive.
Remember: AN OX, RED CAT ‚ÄĒ Anode = Oxidation, Reduction = Cathode. In galvanic cells, anode is negative; in electrolytic, anode is positive.

key_vocabulary.definition: Allows ion flow to maintain electrical neutrality
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Without a salt bridge, the cell would stop working.

key_vocabulary.definition: The voltage of a half-cell measured against the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)

misconceptions.misconception: Students think the anode is always negative.
misconceptions.correction: In a galvanic cell, the anode IS negative. But in an electrolytic cell, the anode is POSITIVE (connected to + terminal of battery).
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: The signs are different because the energy source is different: chemical (galvanic) vs external battery (electrolytic).

assessment.title: Assessment: Galvanic cells: spontaneous redox reactions, cell notation, standard hydrogen electrode, EMF calculation (E¬įcell = E¬įcathode ‚ąí E¬įanode). Electrolytic cells: non-spontaneous, external voltage, anode/cathode identification. Faraday's laws. Applications: electroplating, refining of copper, chlor-alkali process. Comparison of galvanic and electrolytic cells

items.question: Compare galvanic and electrolytic cells in terms of: energy conversion, spontaneity, and anode polarity.
items.correct_answer: Galvanic: chemical‚Üíelectrical, spontaneous, anode negative. Electrolytic: electrical‚Üíchemical, non-spontaneous, anode positive.

items.question: Compare galvanic and electrolytic cells in terms of: energy conversion, spontaneity, and anode polarity.
items.correct_answer: Galvanic: chemical‚Üíelectrical, spontaneous, anode negative. Electrolytic: electrical‚Üíchemical, non-spontaneous, anode positive.
